query: clinical variables that predict pathologic radiographs in first episode of wheezing

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Lobar Emphysema -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis following surgical resection is excellent in patients with CLE. In a single institution case series, between January 2015 and January 2019, 37 neonates and infants with congenital lobar overinflation were treated, with an average age of 111.43 plus or minus 65.19 days, and 22 were boys. All cases exhibited respiratory distress, and 19 also presented with cyanosis. Fifteen cases had recurrent pneumonia and fever. Clinical findings included diminished breath sounds on the affected side in 30 cases and wheezes in 22 cases. Emphysema was observed in all cases via chest radiograph, and confirmatory chest CT scans were performed. The left upper lobe was affected in 23 cases, the right middle lobe in 7 cases, and the right upper lobe in 7 cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Wheezing(Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Other lung sounds that can be mistaken for or that overlap with wheezes are rhonchi and stridor. Rhonchi share similar characteristics to wheezes, with the main difference being a lower dominant frequency of fewer than 200 MHz. This lower frequency is described as a snoring-like sound. Stridor is a higher-pitched and higher amplitude sound due to turbulent airflow around a region of upper airway obstruction. It is typically an inspiratory sound that is far more pronounced when auscultated over the trachea than the thorax. [14] Possible causes of wheezing include: Allergies Anaphylaxis Asthma Bronchiectasis Bronchiolitis Bronchitis Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) Emphysema Epiglottitis Foreign object Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) Heart failure Lung cancer Drugs (i.e., aspirin) Obstructive sleep apnea Pneumonia Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) Respiratory tract infection Smoking Vocal cord dysfunction

retrieved_docs.title: Supraglottic Airway Obstruction -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis will depend on the etiology and the severity of the airway obstruction.

retrieved_docs.title: Stridor in Children -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for stridor is favorable when promptly and appropriately treated.

retrieved_docs.title: Wheezing(Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: When wheezing is heard, some workup is required because it is an abnormal sound. The first imaging test of choice in a patient with wheezing is a chest x-ray to look for a foreign body or a lesion in the central airway. In the non-acute setting, if asthma is suspected, the next step is to obtain baseline pulmonary function tests with bronchodilator administration. Following this, it may be necessary to perform an airway challenge test with a bronchoconstrictive agent such as methacholine. If the wheezing resolves with a bronchodilation agent, a tumor or mass as the cause is a much less likely consideration. If there is no resolution after a breathing treatment, and a tumor or mass is suspected, then a CT scan of the chest and bronchoscopy may be required if possible malignancy is suspected on CT. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Obstruction -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of airway obstruction depends on the etiology of the condition. Generally, the prognosis of inflammatory and infectious causes is more favorable than that of malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Sounds -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Adventitious sounds reflect the pathology that produces them, so the specific clinical context is critical for accurate interpretation.  For example, crackles and the sound of airways snapping open frequently occur in interstitial lung disease, pulmonary edema from heart failure, and infection.  Later and higher-pitched crackles often represent interstitial lung disease, whereas earlier and lower-pitched crackles tend more toward chronic obstructive lung disease.    Wheezing, the sound of air movement through narrowed airways, occurs in conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive lung disease, and focal masses. Without a specific clinical context, these sounds are fairly nonspecific. The duration, rather than pitch, of wheezing throughout the respiratory cycle is most predictive of the degree of pathology. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Bronchospasm -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Wheezing is a common presenting symptom in the preschool age group. However, the majority of these children do not develop a chronic, persistent form of asthma. The odds of developing early childhood asthma are two to three times higher in infants with one or more episodes of bronchiolitis. [18] Infants requiring hospitalization for bronchiolitis have higher early childhood asthma morbidity. [18] Epidemiologic studies have aided in the development of an Asthma Predictive Index to improve the accuracy of diagnosing children younger than three years old with asthma. The Asthma Predictive Index is considered positive if a child has had at least three episodes of wheezing per year, plus meets either one major criterion (parental asthma, atopic dermatitis, or sensitization to air allergens) or two minor criteria (food allergies, wheezing unrelated to “colds”, or eosinophilia greater than or equal to 4%). A positive Asthma Predictive Index means the child is at a significantly higher risk of having asthma later in life. As high as 76% of toddlers with a positive Asthma Predictive Index will have active asthma symptoms when they are school age. [19] [20] In regards to the prognosis in anaphylaxis, early identification and treatment with epinephrine are critical to stopping the progression of anaphylaxis to respiratory or cardiac arrest and death.

retrieved_docs.title: Bagassosis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: After stopping the patient from contact with the offending bagasse dust, complete resolution of symptoms usually occurs in 1 to 6 months. The chest X-ray returns to normal in 2 to 6 months, while pulmonary function normalizes in 2 to 4 months. [1] [7] [15] The mortality rate of the disease is reported to be 7.5% but is likely much lower as milder forms of the disease go undetected. [15] Patients with evidence of lung fibrosis on lung biopsy have a poorer prognosis than those without such changes. [26] HRCT scan can serve as a tool for predicting prognosis. A retrospective study of 69 patients revealed that the degree of fibrosis on these scans was linked with increased mortality. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Desquamative Interstitial Pneumonia -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Few longitudinal studies have characterized the rate of disease progression in DIP. In one such study, Remy-Jardin et al reported an increase in ground-glass changes, emphysema, and centrilobular nodules from 28% to 42%, 26% to 40%, and 33% to 35%, respectively, in current smokers during a mean follow-up period of 5.5 years. [40] In a radiological follow-up of DIP in a small group of 14 patients, cysts increased, and new honeycombing appeared in 35% of cases, including patients on systemic corticosteroid therapy. [35] Survival rates at 10 years are reported to be approximately 70%. [41] Continuation of smoking is clearly associated with a poorer outcome. [24] [42] Children with DIP tend to have a poorer prognosis, particularly for those with ABCA3 mutations. [43] [44] [45]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Bronchitis -- History and Physical -- Clinical Features in Children
retrieved_docs.content: Children with PBB present with a persistent wet cough of at least 4 weeks. In general, affected children have no evidence of upper respiratory disease like otitis or sinusitis or findings associated with chronic lung disease like digital clubbing or chest wall deformity. Parents may report a history of wheezing, not commonly heard on examination. More often, clinicians auscultate coarse breath sounds or crackles. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Lobar Emphysema -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Lobectomy was performed on 31 patients, with a mean age of 147.58 plus or minus 81.49 days at the time of surgery, and 19 of these patients were male. Postoperative complications occurred in 5 cases, with 2 requiring postoperative ventilation. No morbidity or mortality was reported. Follow-up periods ranged from 3 months to 1 year, and all patients were doing well except for one who was lost to follow-up after 3 months. [7] In mild and moderate cases, nonsurgical conservative management has good results. In patients with severe symptoms, lobectomy has an excellent prognosis, and mortality with surgery is very low. [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Alpha-1 Antitrypsin Mutation(Archived) -- History and Physical -- Pulmonary Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary clinical manifestations are similar to the COPD of other etiologies; however, it has some certain characteristics, such as early onset of symptoms, usually in the third or fourth decade, and on CT, the emphysematous involvement is mainly in the lung base. However, symptoms may not present until later, and the basilar changes may occur along with apical changes, thus making the symptoms or signs of ATTD similar to COPD unrelated to ATTD. Symptoms include dyspnea, cough, wheezing, and upper respiratory tract infections. Some risk factors, like cigarette smoking, exposure to burning biomass materials, and respiratory infections, can aggravate pulmonary symptoms. Such factors increase the unopposed elastase activity of neutrophils, leading to the destruction of lung tissue and, eventually, COPD.

retrieved_docs.title: Tracheoesophageal Fistula -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory complications are also common in children with TEF and esophageal atresia. Severe tracheomalacia and bronchomalacia occur in 10% to 20% of infants. Airway reactivity and instability can lead to life-threatening airway obstruction. [54] A small subset of infants require aortopexy for tracheal stabilization and weaning from mechanical ventilation. Children may exhibit a harsh barking cough, which is characteristic of iatrochemicals. Children may also suffer from recurrent bronchitis and pneumonia, common in up to two-thirds of TEF patients in the first few years of life. [55] If left untreated, recurrent infections or frequent aspiration can lead to irreversible lung damage with bronchiectasis and persistent atelectasis. Wheezing is common in up to 40% of survivors and does not improve with age. Recurrent respiratory symptoms are caused by abnormal airway epithelium, impairs mucociliary clearance of airway secretions. The severity of GERD increases the risk for esophageal strictures and dysmotility, thereby potentiating aspiration in such patients. Rarely, persistence or worsening of symptoms may be due to recurrent TEF. Respiratory morbidity decreases in frequency and severity once children reach late adolescence. [55] Hyperinflation of the lungs, reduced lung volumes, and overall abnormal pulmonary function are common in up to 40% of survivors, but some do not affect children’s daily activities. Management of pulmonary pathology includes tailored use of antibiotics, physiotherapy, and GERD treatment to prevent aspiration. Inhaled bronchodilators and steroids help treat asthmatic symptoms. Serial pulmonary function tests and chest CT scans help monitor patient progress. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Additional indications for chest radiography include patients experiencing symptoms that are difficult to control, fever, chronic purulent sputum production, persistently localized wheezing, hemoptysis, weight loss, clubbing, inspiratory crackles, significant hypoxemia, and moderate or severe airflow obstruction that does not reverse with bronchodilators. High-resolution computed tomography is necessary to clarify any abnormalities noted on chest radiographs or for patients with other suspected conditions that may not be well visualized on routine radiographs.

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Diffuse bilateral opacities and infiltrates classically present on chest radiograph, but these findings could be variable showing lobar, dependent or unilateral opacities. Computed tomography usually shows widespread patchy airspace opacities more evident in the dependent areas.

retrieved_docs.content: The SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with the الأَشعة السينية على الصَدْر, indicating that this particular pattern of signs and symptoms often correlates with findings observed on a chest X‑ray. This relationship guides clinicians to employ chest radiography when the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is present, facilitating the evaluation of underlying thoracic pathology and informing subsequent patient care decisions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ). In patients with alcoholic cirrhosis, chest radiographs frequently show atypical patterns that may reflect complications such as pulmonary edema, infections, or pleural effusions, underscoring the importance of evaluating chest imaging when managing this liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a specific type of lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), indicating that it falls under the broader category of pulmonary disturbances. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians understand that respiratory distress syndrome shares fundamental characteristics with other lung disorders, which can guide accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment strategies, and comprehensive patient care.

Disease entities mapped to the respiratory anatomy include malignant lung hilum neoplasm, cancer of the bronchus (سرطان الشعب الهوائية), and malignant bronchial neoplasm. Imaging findings such as miliary pulmonary infiltrates on chest computed tomography support diagnostic correlation with bronchial carcinoma. Together, these standardized vocabularies integrate anatomical structures, laboratory measurements, chemicals, procedural modalities, disease categories, imaging findings, and social factors across multiple languages, providing a comprehensive framework for consistent data capture, analysis, and multidisciplinary patient care in clinical research and practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: The term “hrudník,” referring to the chest, denotes the anatomical location where pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse can develop. These three thoracic conditions are also linked with “دبيلة,” indicating an association between the clinical manifestations of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse and the entity دبيلة. Understanding that the chest (hrudník) is the site of these pathologies and recognizing their connection to دبيلة is important for accurate diagnosis and effective management of patients presenting with pleural and pulmonary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 583
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is classified under the broader category of الْتِهابِ شعبي and is clinically associated with الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ (pulmonary inflammation). Pulmonary inflammation, in turn, includes the specific subtype التهاب رئوي وليدي as a child condition. Both الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ and تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ (alcoholic emphysema) are clinically associated with فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم (hyperkalemia), indicating that elevated serum potassium may be observed in patients with these respiratory disorders. Additionally, الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية (abnormal chest X‑ray) is clinically associated with تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ, suggesting that radiographic abnormalities often accompany alcoholic emphysema. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which acute bronchitis, various forms of pulmonary inflammation, and alcoholic emphysema are linked to hyperkalemia and specific imaging findings, underscoring the importance of integrated clinical, laboratory, and radiographic assessment in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: The mediastinum, a central subdivision of the thorax, serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower respiratory diseases such as lung and mediastinal abscesses (خراج) and is implicated in mediastinal disorders (اضطراب منصفي) including effusions. Mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية)—encompassing mid‑mediastinal, posterior‑mediastinal, superior‑mediastinal, and calcified variants—along with mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (unilateral right, unilateral left, and bilateral) are key thoracic pathologies. Chest computed tomography (CT), classified under imaging procedures related to the chest and respiratory tract, is the primary diagnostic modality for visualizing these conditions. Standard chest CT, thoracic CT without contrast, and specialized thoracic CT scans (e.g., “thoracic CT scan: mediastinal‑pleural fistula”) function as parent procedures that generate specific findings such as “computed tomography of chest: mediastinal mass,” “mediastinal mass containing calcium,” “mass of posterior mediastinum,” “mass of superior mediastinum,” and “mediastinal effusion.” The chest CT hierarchy also includes pulmonary angiographic imaging as a child study. Closed chest drainage is an interventional procedure linked to the thoracic subdivision and guided by chest CT imaging. Breast CT, while primarily a breast imaging study, is positioned inversely to radiographic breast imaging and is subsumed under the broader chest CT category, reflecting overlap between thoracic and breast imaging. Overall, the network emphasizes the mediastinum as a pivotal structure in lower respiratory disease, abscess formation, mass lesions, and fistulous complications, with chest CT—particularly non‑contrast thoracic protocols—serving as the essential tool for diagnosis and procedural planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2348
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is an inflammatory condition that specifically involves the bronchi, indicating that its pathological location is within the bronchial tree. It is classified as a particular form of the broader Arabic‑described condition “الْتِهابِ شعبي,” serving as a parent category for this type of bronchial inflammation. Understanding acute bronchitis as a bronchi‑located disease and as a subset of “الْتِهابِ شعبي” helps clinicians recognize its anatomical focus and its relationship to related respiratory inflammatory disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 213
retrieved_docs.content: An abscess of the lung and mediastinum (خراج) is classified as an other lower respiratory disease, which is associated with segmental disorder (اضطراب منصفي) and inversely related to segmental mass (كتلة منصفية). The mediastinum, a central compartment of the chest (hrudník), is encompassed by the broader thoracic subdivision, and the chest serves as the site for clinical entities such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, pulmonary collapse, as well as interventions like closed chest drainage. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is linked to the chest, mediastinum, and thoracic subdivision and is the principal imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal pathology. Specific CT protocols—CT angiography of thorax with contrast, computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast, diagnostic thoracic CT without contrast, and diagnostic thoracic CT with subsequent contrast—are narrower forms of ct scan chest. Chest CT (التصوير المقطعي المحوسب للصدر) is the Arabic equivalent of computerized axial tomography and is related to other chest x‑ray as a parent modality. Chest CT directly visualizes mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (right, left, or bilateral), mediastinal masses in the mid, posterior, or superior mediastinum, calcium‑containing mediastinal masses, and mediastinal effusion, all categorized as كتلة منصفية or اضطراب منصفي. The combination of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse is associated with the condition دبيلة, underscoring the clinical relevance of accurate mediastinal imaging for diagnosing complications and guiding patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية are clinically associated with تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ and with تعاطي الكحول. The presence of an abnormal chest X‑ray may therefore suggest underlying alcohol‑related pulmonary pathology, such as alcoholic emphysema, and should prompt clinicians to evaluate the patient’s history of alcohol consumption. Recognizing these associations aids in accurate diagnosis and targeted management of patients with suspected alcohol‑related lung disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1213
retrieved_docs.content: Other lower respiratory disease is associated with خراج (abscess) and is classified as a subtype of اضطراب منصفي. The imaging finding described as computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion is also identified as an instance of اضطراب منصفي. Consequently, both the clinical entity other lower respiratory disease and the radiologic observation of mediastinal effusion share the same broader disorder category, اضطراب منصفي, while the respiratory disease specifically carries a risk of developing an abscess.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: The pleura, the serous membrane that envelops the lungs, is classified as a component of the broader category of اضطراب جنبي (lateral disorder). This hierarchical relationship indicates that pleural conditions are considered within the spectrum of disorders affecting the lateral aspects of the thoracic cavity, underscoring the pleura’s relevance in the diagnosis and management of side‑related thoracic pathologies.
